brief:
GAP/RATIONALE: - Choice between radiofrequency (RF) and cryoablation (Cryo) for AVNRT in children is unsettled; no contemporary head-to-head comparison studies exist in the pediatric population. - Need to know whether energy source affects acute success and AV-block/pacing risk.

DESIGN/SETTING: - Retrospective cohort analysis. - Data source: National Cardiovascular Data Registry IMPACT (Improving Pediatric and Adult Congenital Treatment) Registry. - Enrollment window: April 2016 to March 2019.

POPULATION/SAMPLE: - Subjects 1 to 21 years old undergoing elective first-time slow pathway (SP) modification for AVNRT. - Exclusions: age <1 year, congenital heart disease, >1 ablation target. - Total n = 2,448 patients. - Mean age 13.6 +/- 3.4 years; 60% female. - Described as the largest pediatric study of AVNRT ablation.

INTERVENTION/COMPARISON GROUPS (by ablation energy): 1) RF only; 2) Cryo only; 3) RF switching to Cryo (RF->Cryo); 4) Cryo switching to RF (Cryo->RF). - RF only: 43% (n = 1,046). - Cryo only: 49% (n = 1,201). - RF->Cryo: 6% (n = 135). - Cryo->RF: 3% (n = 66).

OUTCOMES: - Primary outcome: acute ablation failure. - Secondary outcomes: in-hospital adverse events.

KEY RESULTS (exact numbers): - Acute ablation failure overall: 1.3% (n = 33). - No difference by energy source: RF only 1%, Cryo only 1.5%, RF->Cryo 1.5%, Cryo->RF 3%; P = 0.5. - AV block requiring permanent pacemaker: did NOT occur in any group (0). - Transient AV block: 0.4% of the cohort, with no difference by group.

MAIN TAKEAWAY: - RF and Cryo showed comparable, high acute success in pediatric AVNRT SP modification. - AV block was rare and did not result in temporary or permanent pacing. - Longitudinal/follow-up data still needed to compare recurrence risk and late complications between modalities.
